Field Report · Dighi, Pune · Maharashtra
March 29, 2026
Ground Report · Environmental Crisis

Dighi Under Threat: What Pune's Rapid Growth Is Leaving Behind

A field investigation into pollution, unsafe water, waste burning, and neglected infrastructure in one of Pune's fastest-growing peripheral neighbourhoods
Assignment Report Field observations & photography — Dighi, Pune · March 2026

Dighi is a sprawling ward on Pune's northern fringe — home to a mix of planned residential colonies, industrial clusters, and informal settlements that have absorbed wave after wave of migration. On 29 March 2026, I walked its edges at dusk and found a neighbourhood caught between urban ambition and environmental collapse: waterlogged lanes, burning garbage heaps, contaminated water pools, and choked arterial roads. This is what I documented — and what the data says about it.

Issue 01 — Water & Sanitation

Flooded Lanes, Hose Pipes & the Water Equity Problem

The first thing you notice in Dighi's informal settlement pockets is water — but not the kind anyone wants. Narrow lanes lay pooled with overflow from drainage hoses, children navigating around puddles on their way home. Residents were seen manually filling drums from a shared municipal hose stretched across a waterlogged road — a daily routine that doubles as a health hazard.

Left: A resident in Dighi's informal settlement zone at dusk — drainage hoses criss-cross the waterlogged lane behind her.  |  Right: Residents navigate the same flooded stretch, collecting water from a shared municipal hose.

This is not an isolated scene. Pune has 390 slums, of which 121 are non-notified — meaning residents have no formal legal claim to services including water connections (PMC Urban Data Portal, 2022). Over 22% of Pune's population lives in such settlements.

Key Data · Water Access
  • Slum dwellers in Indian cities receive just 5% of the total urban water supply, despite making up over 17% of the urban population (National Sample Survey, 69th Round).
  • USAID's Health of the Urban Poor programme found that drinking water sampled at source in Pune slums was relatively clean — but water sampled at households showed significant faecal coliform contamination due to leaky pipelines and shared taps.
  • In Pune's informal settlements, fecal contamination of piped water supply is worsened by poor drainage — leading to waterlogging that amplifies disease transmission (Oxford Academic, Journal of Infectious Diseases, 2021).
  • Globally, unsafe water and sanitation cause an estimated 829,000 deaths annually and 49.8 million disability-adjusted life years lost (WHO, 2016).
Issue 02 — Solid Waste

The Dump at the Edge of the Neighbourhood

A short walk from the settlement boundary revealed an expansive, unsanctioned dump — a mound of mixed waste: crumpled plastic packaging, shredded textiles, construction debris, organic matter. No bins, no signage, no containment. A tell-tale ash patch suggested the site had been burning for days.

Photo: An open illegal dump site at Dighi's settlement boundary. The mound of mixed, unsegregated waste — including plastics, cloth, and construction debris — extended several metres. There were no barriers, collection bins, or warning signs.

"Pune generates around 2,000 tonnes of solid waste every day — with plastic alone making up 30–32% of that total."

Despite the Solid Waste Management (SWM) Rules 2016 mandating door-to-door collection and scientific disposal, enforcement in peripheral wards like Dighi remains patchy. India's Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) found that more than 40% of India's municipal solid waste is still disposed of in open dumps or unlined landfills (CPCB Annual Report, 2023). Pune's own Uruli Devachi landfill has been operating beyond capacity for years, pushing informal dumping outward into peri-urban zones.

Leachate from such unlined dumps — a toxic cocktail of decomposed organic matter, heavy metals, and plastics — seeps into groundwater. CPCB studies have recorded heavy metal concentrations in groundwater near dump sites that far exceed Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) drinking water limits.

Issue 03 — Air Pollution

Burning the Problem Away — and Into Your Lungs

At the same site, flames were actively consuming a pile of mixed waste — plastics, packaging, organic material — all burning together. This is the cheapest way to "manage" waste when no collection truck comes. But the smoke rising from that pit carries some of the most dangerous chemical compounds known to science.

Photo: Active open burning of mixed solid waste at the Dighi dump site, March 2026. Visible ash from previous burns on the left confirms this is a habitual practice at this location. Burning plastics release dioxins and furans — Group 1 carcinogens.

Open burning of waste is explicitly prohibited under SWM Rules 2016 and the Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981. Yet it continues widely, for a simple reason: the legal alternative — organised waste collection and scientific disposal — has not reached these communities.

Key Data · Air Quality & Burning
  • Open waste burning accounts for approximately 10% of global PM2.5 emissions (WHO, 2021).
  • Burning 1 tonne of mixed MSW can release up to 15 grams of dioxin toxic equivalents — compounds classified as Group 1 carcinogens by IARC (Environmental Science & Technology, 2021).
  • Pune's annual PM2.5 average is 46.2 μg/m³ — more than 9 times the WHO guideline of 5 μg/m³ (IQAir World Air Quality Report, 2023).
  • SAFAR-India (IITM Pune) monitoring data places Pune among 131 'non-attainment cities' under India's National Clean Air Programme (NCAP, 2019) — cities that consistently fail National Ambient Air Quality Standards.
  • The National Clean Air Programme targets a 20–30% reduction in particulate matter in these cities by 2024 — a target Pune is yet to meet (IQAir, 2024).
Issue 04 — Water Contamination

A Pool of Poison: Sewage, Plastics & Stagnant Water

Further into the wasteland bordering the settlement, a shallow, stagnant pool sat choked with plastic waste and debris — a broken drainage pipe discharging what appeared to be grey water or sewage directly into an open area surrounded by vegetation. The water was visibly turbid and discoloured.

Photo: A stagnant, contaminated water pool at the Dighi site boundary. A broken metal pipe (foreground) appears to be discharging drainage water into the open area. Plastic waste and debris are visible throughout the pool. Such sites breed disease vectors and contaminate groundwater.

This is a microcosm of Pune's larger water crisis. The city's two primary rivers, the Mula and Mutha, receive untreated sewage at scale. The Centre for Science and Environment (CSE) documented that while Pune has an installed sewage treatment capacity of 599 MLD, the sewage load far exceeds this — and a significant portion enters rivers untreated.

Key Data · Water Contamination
  • The Mula-Mutha river's Biological Oxygen Demand (BOD) — a key indicator of organic pollution — has been recorded at over 30 mg/L, more than ten times the 3 mg/L permissible limit for safe bathing (CSE, Excreta Matters Report).
  • A 2025 study published in Science of the Total Environment found microplastic concentrations of 1,808 ± 697 particles/litre in the Mula River's surface water during pre-monsoon — with packaging materials as the dominant polymer (ScienceDirect, 2025).
  • Approximately 33% of Pune's sewage remains untreated before entering rivers and groundwater systems, despite the city's installed treatment capacity (CSE India, Excreta Matters).
  • Contaminated water is responsible for over 800,000 deaths globally each year — predominantly children under five from diarrhoeal diseases (WHO).
Issue 05 — Urban Traffic

Five Million Vehicles, One Choked City

A few kilometres away — the same evening — the contrast sharpens. Dighi merges into Pune's urban arterial network: a busy intersection at dusk, thick with motorcycles, autorickshaws, trucks, tempos. No cycle tracks. No safe pedestrian crossings visible. Overhead wires sag under the weight of a city that grew faster than its planning.

Photo: Evening traffic at a major road junction near Dighi, Pune — March 2026. Mixed heavy and light traffic with no dedicated cycling or pedestrian infrastructure. Vehicular emissions contribute ~28% of Pune's PM2.5 load (SAFAR-India, IITM Pune).

Pune has crossed 5 million registered vehicles as of 2024 (Pune RTO), making it one of the most vehicle-dense cities in India relative to its road network. The billboard in the background advertising new housing towers captures the city's contradiction perfectly: construction booms while the environment buckles.

Summary

The Numbers at a Glance

Issue Observed Key Metric Figure Source
Solid Waste Generation Daily MSW in Pune ~2,000 tonnes/day PMC / AQI.in, 2024
Open Dumping MSW in open dumps (India) >40% of total CPCB Annual Report, 2023
Untreated Sewage Proportion untreated in Pune ~33% CSE Excreta Matters
River Water Quality BOD in Mula-Mutha >30 mg/L (limit: 3 mg/L) CSE India
Microplastics in Mula River Surface water concentration 1,808 ± 697 particles/L ScienceDirect, 2025
Air Quality (PM2.5) Annual average, Pune 46.2 μg/m³ (WHO limit: 5 μg/m³) IQAir World Report, 2023
Slum Population % of Pune population in slums >22% PMC Urban Data Portal, 2022
Registered Vehicles Pune city total 5 million+ Pune RTO, 2024
Conclusion

Dighi Is Not the Exception — It Is the Rule

What I documented in Dighi on 29 March 2026 is not unique to this neighbourhood. It is the lived reality of Pune's peripheral growth zones — areas that absorb thousands of new residents every year but receive a fraction of the civic infrastructure investment that central Pune enjoys.

The co-location of five environmental problems — waterlogging, inadequate sanitation, open waste dumping, unregulated burning, and sewage contamination — in a single neighbourhood creates a compounding toxic burden on residents who are already economically vulnerable. The children playing in those waterlogged lanes breathe dioxin smoke, drink from potentially contaminated pipes, and have no green buffer from a city that is growing faster than its conscience.

"Environmental degradation in Dighi is not an accident. It is the outcome of decades of unplanned growth, under-investment in peripheral infrastructure, and weak enforcement of laws that exist on paper."

What needs to happen: Rigorous enforcement of SWM Rules 2016 to end open dumping and burning in peri-urban zones; expansion of sewage treatment to achieve 100% coverage; targeted PMC investment in Dighi-area drainage infrastructure; and ward-level accountability mechanisms so that environmental complaints translate into action — not just paperwork.

These are not expensive asks. They are legal obligations already on the books. The question is political will.
